description: Woke up to no water in the kitchen. Turned on the faucet and heard a weird gurgling sound but nothing came out. Checked under the sink and found a thin sheet of ice on the pipe and a small crack where water was slowly dripping.
resolution: Kitchen cold water supply pipe froze and developed a hairline crack at the elbow joint. Thawed the line with a heat gun and replaced the cracked 3/4-inch copper section with a new coupling. Insulated exposed pipe run under the sink with foam sleeves.
notes: First winter issue. Building has minimal pipe insulation — 1960s construction. Mike mentioned this could become a recurring problem.

description: The toilet in the bathroom runs constantly. You can hear it cycling on and off every few minutes. It's been doing this for about a week, but it seems to be getting worse.
resolution: Replaced deteriorated flapper valve in toilet tank. Old flapper had visible warping and mineral buildup preventing proper seal. Installed Korky universal flapper. Tested flush cycle — holding seal properly now.
notes: Toilet is a 1998 American Standard. Functional but aging. Flapper valve is a wear item — expect replacement every 2-3 years.

description: The outlet next to the bathroom sink keeps tripping. I reset it and it works for about an hour, then trips again. I need to use my hair dryer and electric toothbrush and neither will stay on.
resolution: Bathroom GFCI outlet was tripping due to moisture intrusion in the outlet box from condensation. Replaced GFCI receptacle and applied silicone sealant around the box edges. Tested with load — holding steady at 120V, trips properly on test button.
notes: Rosa noted the bathroom has no exhaust fan — moisture is a persistent issue. Recommended installing a vent fan. Owner declined for now.

description: The garbage disposal is making a terrible grinding noise and won't drain. Something is definitely stuck in there.
resolution: Retrieved a metal tablespoon lodged between the impeller blades and the grind ring. Disposal motor and blades are undamaged. Ran water test — draining normally. Advised tenant to run water before and during disposal use.
notes: No charge — Mike was already in the building for another unit. Found a tablespoon. Mystery solved.

description: The smoke detector in the hallway went off while I was cooking breakfast. It does this every time I use the stove. The kitchen doesn't even have a door to close. Had to wave a towel at it for ten minutes.
resolution: Smoke detector is a photoelectric unit mounted 8 feet from the kitchen. Relocated detector to a position 15 feet from the stove per NFPA guidelines. Tested with canned smoke — responds appropriately at new location.
notes: Original placement was terrible — practically above the stove. Previous maintenance company must have just slapped it wherever. Moved to hallway ceiling near bedroom.

description: I can't get my front door to lock properly. The deadbolt won't fully extend — it sticks about halfway. I had to wedge a chair under the doorknob to feel safe sleeping tonight.
resolution: Deadbolt strike plate misaligned due to seasonal wood swelling in the door frame. Chiseled out the strike plate mortise by 1/8 inch and reinstalled. Lubricated the deadbolt mechanism with graphite powder. Lock now engages fully and smoothly.
notes: Mike is a plumber but also a handy guy. Door frame is original 1960s wood — swells every summer. May need to replace the frame eventually.

description: The kitchen faucet has been dripping steadily for about two weeks. Not a huge amount but it's constant and the drip-drip-drip is driving me crazy at night.
resolution: Replaced worn ceramic disc cartridge in the kitchen faucet. Old cartridge had a chipped seal causing a steady 2-drip-per-second leak. Installed OEM replacement cartridge. No drip on close — tested hot and cold sides.
notes: Faucet is a no-name brand, hard to find parts. Mike had to bring a universal cartridge. Works fine for now.

description: I plugged in the toaster and microwave at the same time on the kitchen counter and there was a visible spark from the outlet. The outlet is now black around the plug holes and smells like burning plastic.
resolution: Kitchen counter outlet showed signs of arcing and heat damage on the receptacle face. The outlet was wired with original 1960s aluminum branch wiring connected to a 15A breaker, feeding three outlets on one circuit. Replaced the damaged outlet with a new 20A-rated receptacle with CO/ALR rating for aluminum wiring compatibility. Recommended a dedicated kitchen circuit — owner deferred.
notes: Rosa flagged aluminum wiring throughout the unit as a concern. Three kitchen outlets share a single 15A circuit, which is under-spec for modern appliance loads. Upgrade to 20A circuit recommended. Owner says 'it's been fine for 60 years.'

description: The toilet is acting up again. This time the tank takes forever to fill after flushing — like 5 minutes of running water. Sometimes it just doesn't stop filling at all.
resolution: Fill valve assembly failed — the float was waterlogged and not rising to shut off the inlet. Replaced the entire fill valve assembly with a Fluidmaster 400A. Adjusted the water level to the marked fill line. Tank now refills in approximately 45 seconds.
notes: Second toilet issue in 7 months. Mike says the toilet is functional but all the internals are reaching end of life simultaneously. Suggested a full rebuild kit ($200) or replacement ($400-500). Owner elected to fix only the immediate issue.

description: The heater isn't blowing warm air. It turns on and the fan runs, but the air coming out is room temperature at best. The apartment is 54 degrees and it's freezing.
resolution: Wall heater igniter was cracked and not producing spark to light the gas burner. Replaced the hot surface igniter and cleaned the burner assembly of dust and debris. Tested through three heating cycles — igniting consistently and producing 120F supply air. Gas valve and thermocouple tested normal.
notes: James from CoolBreeze noted the wall heater is original to the building — a 1960s Williams wall furnace. Parts are getting harder to source. May need full replacement within 2-3 years.

description: The dishwasher stopped mid-cycle and won't start again. The control panel lights up but nothing happens when I press start. There's still standing water in the bottom.
resolution: Door latch switch was faulty — the dishwasher thought the door was open. Replaced the door latch assembly and manually drained the standing water from the tub. Ran a complete wash cycle — unit completed normally. Inspected the drain hose for kinks — none found.
notes: Dishwasher is a 2015 model. Door latch is a common failure point on this model. Should be fine for now.

description: Water is dripping from under the bathroom sink onto the floor. I noticed it at 3am when I stepped in a puddle going to the bathroom. There's about a quarter inch of water on the tile.
resolution: PVC drain joint under the bathroom sink failed at the P-trap connection. The compression nut had cracked, allowing water to seep out during any sink use. Replaced the P-trap assembly and the PVC tailpiece with new Schedule 40 fittings. Tested for 10 minutes of continuous flow — no leaks.
notes: Winter pipe issue #2. The PVC under the bathroom sink is original-ish — probably late 1980s retrofit. Mike says the whole drain assembly should be replaced proactively but owner only wants to fix what's broken.

description: Kitchen GFCI outlet tripped and I can't reset it. It keeps clicking but won't hold. My coffee maker and toaster are plugged in there and I need my morning coffee.
resolution: GFCI receptacle had reached end of life — internal test mechanism failed. These units are rated for about 15-25 years and this one appears original to a 1990s kitchen update. Replaced with a new Leviton GFCI receptacle. Tested with plug-in tester — trips and resets correctly.
notes: Rosa says the kitchen wiring is a time capsule of every decade's electrical code. She found knob-and-tube in one wall and romex in the next. 'Creative' is the word she used.

description: The garbage disposal is making that terrible grinding noise again. Last time there was a spoon, but I've checked and there's nothing visible inside. It grinds for a second and then just hums.
resolution: Motor bearings on the garbage disposal are shot. Unit was cycling between grinding and stalling, drawing excessive current. No foreign objects found. Replaced the entire InSinkErator Badger 5 disposal unit with a new 1/2 HP model. Reconnected dishwasher drain knockout and tested with water flow.
notes: Tenant swears there's nothing in the disposal this time. Mike confirmed — no foreign objects. Motor just gave out. New disposal installed, should last 8-10 years.

description: There's water on the bathroom floor around the base of the toilet. It's not a lot but the floor is wet every time someone flushes. The grout around the toilet base is discolored and there's a musty smell.
resolution: Wax ring seal between the toilet base and the floor flange had deteriorated, allowing sewer water to seep onto the floor with each flush. Pulled the toilet, scraped off the old wax, inspected the flange (intact but corroded), and installed a new Hercules Johni-Ring wax gasket. Reset the toilet with new closet bolts and hand-tightened. Tested with five flushes — completely dry at the base.
notes: Third toilet issue in 13 months. Water damage to the subfloor is minimal but if this keeps happening the floor under the toilet will need replacing. Mike is starting to know this toilet personally.

description: The bedroom window has condensation between the panes every morning. There's also a noticeable cold draft coming from the bottom of the window. The seal around the frame is cracked and crumbly.
resolution: Bedroom window IGU (insulated glass unit) seal has failed, allowing moisture between the double panes. Re-caulked the exterior frame perimeter with DAP Dynaflex 230 and installed foam weatherstrip along the bottom sash rail to reduce air infiltration. The IGU itself needs full replacement to eliminate the condensation but the draft is resolved for now.
notes: Windows are original single-pane with storm windows added later (probably 1980s). The storm window IGU seal is blown. Full window replacement would be $600-$800 per unit but owner opted for weatherstripping only.

description: The AC stopped working in the middle of a heat wave. It was 98 degrees outside yesterday and now the apartment is at 89. The unit hums for a second when I turn it on but the compressor never kicks in.
resolution: AC compressor start/run capacitor failed. Measured 8 microfarads on a 35 microfarad rated capacitor — well below operating spec. Replaced with a new 35/5 MFD 440V dual run capacitor. Compressor started immediately and began cooling. Supply air measured 58F after 15 minutes. Cleaned condenser coils while on site — moderate dust buildup.
notes: James from CoolBreeze says the AC unit is a 2010 install and the capacitor is a common failure point in Bay Area heat. Capacitors last about 10-15 years. Should be good for a while.

description: Two outlets in the living room suddenly stopped working. My TV and lamp just went dark. I checked the breaker panel and nothing looks tripped. Tried resetting every breaker anyway — still dead.
resolution: Found a failed backstab connection on the first outlet in the daisy chain. The 14-gauge wire had worked loose from the push-in terminal over time, breaking the circuit to both downstream outlets. Removed all backstab connections on this circuit and rewired using screw terminals on all three outlets. Tested all outlets — 120V confirmed.
notes: Rosa says backstab wiring connections are the bane of her existence. The whole unit is wired with them. She recommends re-terminating every outlet to screw terminals proactively. Owner: 'If it ain't broke...'

description: Smoke detector went off again while I was making stir fry. It's the relocated one from last year. I had all the windows open and the stove fan running. This thing is way too sensitive.
resolution: Smoke detector is functioning as designed — cooking smoke is reaching the detector even at its relocated position. Installed a photoelectric-type detector with a hush button to replace the ionization-type detector, which is more sensitive to cooking particles. Tenant instructed on hush button usage.
notes: Second false alarm. The apartment layout just doesn't give smoke anywhere to go. New photoelectric unit should be less sensitive to cooking smoke. Rosa also pointed out there's no range hood exhaust — just a recirculating fan that does basically nothing.

description: There's a yellowish-brown stain on the living room ceiling that appeared over the past week. It's about 18 inches across and seems to be getting larger. The ceiling is slightly soft when I press on it.
resolution: Investigated the unit above (4B) — found a slow leak from the upstairs bathroom supply line condensation dripping onto the shared ceiling joist. Applied KILZ primer to the stained area after allowing it to dry fully, then painted with ceiling white to match. Coordinated with upstairs unit maintenance to insulate the sweating pipe.
notes: Not our plumbing — the upstairs unit's condensation is dripping through. Painted over it for now. Upstairs tenant was notified. This stain has a way of coming back.

description: I woke up at 2am to a loud hissing sound coming from the closet where the water heater is. There's water spraying out of a valve on the side of the tank and the closet floor is soaked. Water is spreading into the hallway.
resolution: Water heater temperature and pressure (T&P) relief valve discharged due to excessive tank pressure — likely caused by thermal expansion with no expansion tank installed and a check valve on the main line preventing backflow. Replaced the T&P relief valve, drained and flushed the tank sediment, and installed a 2-gallon thermal expansion tank on the cold water inlet. Emergency 2am service call rate applied.
notes: 2am emergency call. Mike was not thrilled. The water heater had no expansion tank — a code violation in most jurisdictions. The check valve on the main was trapping thermal expansion pressure. Mike installed an expansion tank and strongly recommended a water heater replacement within 1-2 years. Tank is 14 years old.

description: The toilet flush handle is loose and floppy. You have to jiggle it just right to get it to flush, and sometimes the chain inside gets caught and the toilet runs until you open the tank and unhook it manually.
resolution: Flush handle mounting nut was stripped and the lift chain had two broken links causing it to hang unevenly. Replaced the flush handle assembly and installed a new stainless steel lift chain with an adjustable clip. Set chain slack to 1/2 inch per spec. Flush engages cleanly on first press now.
notes: Fourth toilet repair in under two years. At this point we've replaced the flapper, fill valve, wax ring, and now the handle/chain. The only original parts left are the tank, bowl, and our collective patience.

description: The lights in the kitchen flicker whenever the refrigerator compressor kicks on. It's been doing it for a few months but now it's more noticeable. The microwave clock also resets sometimes.
resolution: Refrigerator and kitchen lights share a 15A branch circuit that is undersized for the combined load. When the compressor inrush current spikes, it causes voltage drop on the shared circuit resulting in flickering. Tightened all panel connections and checked for loose neutral — found the neutral bus bar had a slightly loose connection. Tightened to spec. Flickering reduced but not eliminated. Dedicated fridge circuit is the proper fix.
notes: Rosa opened up the panel and said, and I quote, 'This is a museum of creative 1960s wiring decisions.' The panel is a Federal Pacific Stab-Lok — known fire hazard. She strongly recommends a full panel replacement. Owner is 'considering it.'

description: Front door deadbolt is stuck again. This time it's jammed while locked — I literally cannot open my front door from the inside. I had to climb out the bedroom window to get to work this morning.
resolution: Deadbolt cylinder had seized due to internal pin corrosion. The tenant forced the lock attempting to open it, which bent the tailpiece. Drilled out the jammed cylinder, replaced the entire deadbolt assembly with a new Schlage B60N, and re-keyed to match the existing key system. Door frame strike plate re-mortised to align with new bolt.
notes: Second deadbolt issue. Tenant had to exit via window. Not ideal. The original lock was probably installed when disco was popular. New Schlage should last a decade if maintained.

description: The bathtub is draining incredibly slowly. Water pools up to my ankles during a shower. I've tried drain cleaner and a plunger but nothing helps.
resolution: Snaked the bathtub drain with a 1/4-inch cable — encountered heavy hair and soap buildup at approximately 4 feet into the drain line, just past the P-trap. Cleared the blockage and flushed with hot water. Drain now flowing at full rate. Installed a mesh drain cover to reduce future hair ingestion.
notes: Standard drain clog. Nothing unusual. Installed a drain hair catcher for $3 that will prevent this from happening again. Sometimes the cheapest fix is the best fix.

description: The ceiling light fixture in the bedroom is buzzing loudly and the light flickers on and off. I replaced the bulbs but it still does it.
resolution: Ceiling fixture had a corroded socket and a loose wire nut on the hot lead inside the junction box. Replaced the fixture socket and re-made all wire connections with new wire nuts and electrical tape. Installed LED bulbs. No buzzing or flickering on test.
notes: The wire nuts inside the box were the old ceramic type from the original install. Rosa is running out of diplomatic ways to say 'this wiring needs to be redone.'

description: The dishwasher is leaking badly. Water is pouring out from under the front of the unit during the wash cycle. I came home and the kitchen floor was flooded. The tenant in 2B downstairs just called and said water is dripping through their ceiling.
resolution: Dishwasher door gasket had split along the bottom, allowing wash water to flow out the front during the spray cycle. Water penetrated through the subfloor and dripped into Unit 2B below, damaging a 2x3 foot section of their ceiling drywall. Replaced the door gasket, dried and inspected the subfloor (minor swelling but structurally sound), and coordinated a ceiling patch repair for Unit 2B.
notes: Dishwasher flooded through to the unit below. Cost includes door gasket replacement ($185) and ceiling drywall repair for 2B ($240). The 2B tenant was not happy. Dishwasher is now 8 years old — these gaskets typically last 10 years, so this was premature. Possibly related to the door latch replacement from 2021 — may have affected gasket alignment.

description: There's a slow drip behind the washing machine. I pulled it out from the wall and can see a tiny bead of water forming on one of the hoses where it connects to the wall.
resolution: Washing machine hot water supply hose had a pinhole leak at the crimp fitting where the hose meets the brass connector. Rubber hose was showing age cracking. Replaced both hot and cold supply hoses with braided stainless steel lines to prevent future failures. Tightened connections with channel locks — no leaks on pressure test.
notes: Rubber washing machine hoses should be replaced every 5 years. These were original to the washer install (unknown age). Stainless braided lines are much more durable. Crisis averted — a burst washer hose can dump 500 gallons per hour.

description: The AC is dead again. Same thing as last summer — it hums but the compressor won't start. It's 95 degrees outside and my apartment feels like a sauna. This is the second summer in a row this has happened.
resolution: Start/run capacitor failed again — same failure mode as July 2022. Measured 5 microfarads on a 35 MFD rated capacitor. The capacitor is located in the condensing unit which sits in direct afternoon sun on a west-facing wall, which accelerates thermal degradation. Replaced with a new Titan Pro 35/5 MFD 440V capacitor. Compressor started immediately. Supply air at 56F after 10 minutes.
notes: Same exact capacitor failure as last year. James says the condensing unit gets full afternoon sun and heat-soaks the capacitor. Recommended a shade structure over the condenser or at minimum a higher-rated capacitor. Owner said no to the shade structure. James installed the same spec capacitor and said 'see you next summer.'

description: The toilet is running AGAIN. Same constant-running sound as 2021. I know the drill at this point — it's the flapper. Please send Mike.
resolution: Replaced the flapper valve. Again. I was literally just here for this two and a half years ago. Same toilet, same failure, same part. The tank interior has significant mineral scaling that is degrading the flapper seat surface, which is why these keep failing prematurely. Recommended either a toilet rebuild kit with a new flush valve seat or a full toilet replacement. Tenant is correct — it is definitely the flapper.
notes: Mike's exact words in his invoice notes: 'I was literally just here for this.' This is the fifth toilet repair since March 2021. The mineral scaling on the flush valve seat is chewing through flappers. A new toilet would be $400-500 installed and would end this cycle. Owner: 'Just replace the flapper.'

description: The garbage disposal is making that grinding noise again. I know you replaced it last year. There is NOTHING in there — I checked with a flashlight and my hand (with it off, don't worry). It just randomly grinds and vibrates loudly for a few seconds then stops.
resolution: Inspected the disposal thoroughly — no foreign objects, no loose components, mounting ring is tight, splash guard intact. Ran the disposal with water for 3 minutes — intermittent grinding sound that I cannot reproduce on demand. Motor sounds fine, impellers are free. I have no explanation. This disposal is haunted.
notes: Third disposal grinding incident. First time: spoon. Second time: motor failure, replaced unit. Third time: ??? Mike literally could not find anything wrong. His invoice says 'This disposal is haunted.' I am not going to argue with him.

description: The bathroom outlet is tripping again. We just replaced it last year! This time it trips whenever I plug in anything — even just my phone charger.
resolution: GFCI outlet itself is functioning correctly — the issue is downstream. Traced the circuit and found a junction box in the wall cavity behind the medicine cabinet with a corroded wire splice that was creating a ground fault under load. Repaired the splice with a proper wire nut connection and wrapped with electrical tape. GFCI now holds under load.
notes: The hidden junction box behind the medicine cabinet had wire splices wrapped in cloth electrical tape — vintage 1960s craftsmanship. Rosa found it by tracing the circuit. She says every time she opens a wall in this unit she finds something new and terrifying.

description: That ceiling stain in the living room is back. Same spot as last year. It's smaller this time but definitely yellowish and damp to the touch.
resolution: Ceiling stain recurrence in the same location as October 2022. Inspected unit 4B above — the previously insulated pipe is still sweating through the wrap during temperature swings. Applied a second layer of closed-cell foam insulation to the pipe in 4B and re-primed and painted the ceiling stain in 3B with KILZ Original oil-based primer and one coat of ceiling paint.
notes: The ghost stain returns. Same spot, same cause, same fix. Mike says until the pipe in 4B is properly insulated with a vapor barrier (not just foam wrap), this will keep happening every fall when the temperature differentials cause condensation. Filed under: we'll see you next October.

description: The thermostat display is blank and the heater won't turn on. I've tried turning it off and on. The batteries are fresh — I just replaced them. The apartment is getting cold.
resolution: Thermostat was fine — the issue was a tripped circuit breaker for the HVAC system. Breaker had tripped due to a momentary short in the thermostat wiring where it passes through a wall stud. The wire insulation had been nicked (probably during original installation 60 years ago) and finally made contact with the stud nail. Repaired the nicked wire with heat shrink, reset the breaker, and confirmed heating operation through a full cycle.
notes: James found thermostat wire that had been nicked by a nail for presumably 60 years and finally wore through. He said it's a miracle it didn't short sooner. This building is held together by luck and inertia.

description: The kitchen sink is backed up and won't drain at all. I was washing Thanksgiving dishes and the water just stopped going down. Both sides of the sink are full of standing water.
resolution: Kitchen drain backed up with grease and food debris — classic post-Thanksgiving clog. Snaked the drain line with a 3/8-inch cable and cleared a grease plug approximately 8 feet downstream at the main stack connection. Flushed with hot water and enzyme-based drain cleaner. Both sink basins draining freely. Advised tenant to avoid pouring grease down the drain.
notes: Annual Thanksgiving grease clog. Mike says this happens to half his clients the week after Thanksgiving. Standard maintenance.

description: The outlet in the living room where I have my space heater plugged in is hot to the touch. Like, uncomfortably warm. The outlet cover plate feels warm and there's a faint burning smell. I unplugged everything and I'm not touching it.
resolution: Outlet had a high-resistance connection on the hot terminal — the screw was finger-tight instead of torqued, and the wire was showing heat discoloration. Under the sustained load of a space heater (1500W on a 15A circuit), the loose connection was generating significant heat. Replaced the outlet, properly torqued all terminals, and inspected adjacent outlets on the same circuit. Recommended tenant use a lower-wattage heater or use the wall furnace instead.
notes: This could have been a fire. Rosa is now officially concerned about this unit's electrical system. Loose connections, aluminum wiring, Federal Pacific panel, backstab terminals — she wrote a formal recommendation letter for a full electrical upgrade. Owner received it. No response yet.

description: Woke up to water dripping from under the bathroom sink again — same spot as January 2022. There's a puddle on the floor and the cabinet under the sink is wet. I put a towel down but it soaked through in an hour.
resolution: Same PVC drain joint under the bathroom sink failed again at the P-trap connection — the exact same failure as January 2022. The replacement compression fitting from two years ago cracked in the same location. The issue is that the drain stack has a slight misalignment that puts lateral stress on the P-trap joint. Replaced the P-trap assembly again with a new fitting. I told you this whole section needs replacing — the entire drain line from the sink to the stack should be re-routed to eliminate the stress point.
notes: Mike is frustrated. Same exact PVC joint, same exact failure, exactly two years later. He wrote on his invoice: 'I told you this whole section needs replacing.' The drain stack misalignment is putting stress on the joint. A proper fix would be $800-1200 to re-route the drain line. Owner deferred again.

description: There's a crack in the hallway wall that I swear wasn't there before. It runs diagonally from the corner of the door frame up toward the ceiling, about two feet long. The paint is flaking along the crack.
resolution: Diagonal drywall crack originating from the door frame corner — classic stress crack from building settling. Routed out the crack to 1/8 inch width, applied fiberglass mesh tape, then three coats of joint compound with sanding between coats. Primed with KILZ and applied two coats of matching wall paint. No structural concern — cosmetic only.
notes: Building settling crack. Normal for a 60+ year old building. The foundation has probably been settling since before the tenant was born. Cosmetic repair only.

description: The refrigerator is making a loud clicking sound every few minutes and the inside temperature is climbing. My food is getting warm — the milk is lukewarm. The freezer still seems cold but the fridge section is definitely not keeping up.
resolution: Compressor start relay was failing — creating the clicking sound as it tried and failed to start the compressor. Replaced the PTC start relay and the overload protector. Compressor now starts and runs continuously. Fridge temperature dropped from 52F to 37F within two hours. Freezer was maintaining due to thermal mass but would have failed eventually. Cleaned the condenser coils while on site — heavy dust accumulation was contributing to compressor strain.
notes: Fridge is about 10 years old. Start relay failure is common at this age. Dirty condenser coils were making it work harder. Told tenant to pull it out and vacuum the coils every 6 months. They nodded politely in a way that means they will never do that.

description: The air coming out of the vents smells musty, like a basement. It's been bugging me for a few weeks and it's getting worse now that we're using the heater less and the AC more.
resolution: Inspected the ductwork and found mold growth on the interior surface of the supply plenum where condensation had accumulated over winter. Cleaned the accessible ductwork with antimicrobial spray, replaced the air filter (which was black — hasn't been changed in at least a year), and cleaned the evaporator coil with foaming coil cleaner. Air quality immediately improved.
notes: James pulled out the air filter and it was legitimately black. He held it up and said 'When was the last time this was changed?' The answer is apparently never, or at least not since the last tenant. Added quarterly filter changes to the maintenance schedule.

description: The bathroom faucet handle is really stiff and hard to turn. The hot side barely moves and when it does, it makes a squealing noise like a wounded animal.
resolution: Bathroom faucet hot-side stem cartridge was calcified with mineral deposits. Disassembled the faucet, soaked the cartridge in CLR for 20 minutes, cleaned the valve seat, and applied waterproof silicone grease to the O-rings and stem. Handle now turns smoothly. Cold side is starting to show the same buildup — will probably need attention within a year.
notes: San Francisco water is hard on fixtures. The mineral deposits in this building are aggressive. Mike says the whole building probably needs a water softener, which is approximately never going to happen.

description: The light switch in the bathroom makes a crackling/popping sound when I flip it on. It doesn't do it every time but maybe 1 out of 3 times. I can also see a tiny spark through the crack in the switch plate when it happens.
resolution: Light switch contacts were pitted and arcing internally. The switch is an original toggle-type from the building's construction — approximately 60 years of service. Replaced with a new 15A toggle switch. Inspected the wire connections inside the box — hot wire showed some heat discoloration at the terminal but is otherwise sound. Installed a new nylon switch plate to replace the cracked old one.
notes: A 60-year-old light switch finally gave up. Rosa says she's surprised it lasted this long. There are probably 10 more switches in the unit from the same era. We'll replace them as they fail. Or all at once if the owner ever approves Rosa's electrical upgrade proposal, which has been sitting in his inbox since December.

description: The grout between the bathroom floor tiles is crumbling and falling out. Some tiles near the toilet are slightly loose and move when you step on them. The floor feels spongy in that area.
resolution: Bathroom floor grout has deteriorated around the toilet area — likely accelerated by the previous wax ring leak incidents. Removed loose grout, cleaned joints, and re-grouted approximately 15 square feet of tile with polymer-modified sanded grout. Three tiles were loose due to deteriorated thinset — removed, cleaned, and re-set with new thinset. Applied grout sealer after 72-hour cure. The subfloor in this area has minor water damage but is still structurally adequate.
notes: Consequence of the repeated toilet wax ring and PVC drain leaks — water has been getting under these tiles intermittently for years. The subfloor is showing early signs of rot. If we don't address the root cause (the drain stack misalignment and the aging toilet), the entire bathroom floor will eventually need to be torn out and rebuilt.

description: The microwave turntable stopped spinning. The microwave heats food but unevenly since the plate doesn't rotate anymore. It also makes a buzzing sound that it didn't used to make.
resolution: Microwave turntable motor burned out. Replaced the synchronous turntable motor — a common wear item on this model. Also found the turntable coupler was cracked, so replaced that as well. Turntable now rotates smoothly and the buzzing sound is gone.
notes: Minor appliance repair. Microwave is a basic GE over-the-range model, probably 2012-2015 vintage. Still heating fine — just needed a $15 motor. Total with labor is $110 because SF electrician rates are what they are.

description: The hot water runs out way faster than it used to. I can get maybe 5 minutes of hot water in the shower before it goes lukewarm. It used to last at least 15 minutes.
resolution: Water heater has significant sediment buildup reducing effective tank capacity. Drained and flushed the tank — approximately 3 gallons of sediment-laden water came out before running clear. Also found the lower heating element (electric unit) had a thick scale coating reducing efficiency. Descaled the element and flushed the tank. Hot water recovery improved significantly. Tank is 16 years old — well past typical 10-12 year lifespan.
notes: This water heater is 16 years old and living on borrowed time. Mike drained it and the sediment that came out looked like beach sand. He says it could fail catastrophically at any time and recommended immediate replacement ($1,200-$1,800 installed). Owner said to 'keep nursing it along.' Mike visibly sighed.

description: Not an emergency, but the wall heater is making a rattling sound even when it's off. There's a faint vibration coming from it. Sounds like something is loose inside.
resolution: Wall heater draft hood was loose — a mounting screw had vibrated out and the hood was resonating with building vibrations (probably from HVAC or laundry equipment elsewhere in the building). Re-secured the draft hood with new screws and added a foam gasket to eliminate vibration transfer. Also tightened the gas line connection which had loosened slightly. No gas leak detected with combustible gas detector.
notes: Minor heater maintenance. James tightened everything up. The gas line connection being slightly loose is worth noting — he says it wasn't leaking but it needs to be monitored. These old wall furnaces are basically antiques at this point.

description: The breaker for the bedroom circuit keeps tripping. I've unplugged everything in the bedroom and it still trips within a few hours. There's a burning smell near the panel when it trips.
resolution: Circuit breaker for the bedroom circuit (Breaker #7 in Federal Pacific panel) was failing to hold due to internal contact degradation. This is a known issue with Federal Pacific Stab-Lok breakers. Replaced the individual breaker with an aftermarket Connecticut Electric UBIF replacement rated for Stab-Lok panels. However, I must reiterate my strong recommendation to replace this entire panel — Federal Pacific Stab-Lok panels have a documented failure rate and are considered a fire hazard by multiple industry studies.
notes: Rosa is now using words like 'liability' and 'fire hazard' in her reports. The Federal Pacific panel is a ticking time bomb. She's replaced individual breakers three times now but the root cause is the panel itself. Full panel upgrade would be $2,500-$4,000. Owner finally said he'll 'look into it next quarter.' We've been hearing that since 2023.

description: I cannot believe this but the ceiling stain is back. AGAIN. Same exact spot in the living room. Third year in a row. At this point I think the ceiling is trying to tell me something.
resolution: Third recurrence of the living room ceiling stain from the sweating pipe in Unit 4B. This time I cut a 12-inch inspection hole in the ceiling to directly assess the pipe and found the foam insulation wrap was completely saturated and had become a moisture sponge rather than a barrier. Removed the wet insulation, installed proper foil-faced fiberglass pipe insulation with vapor barrier facing and sealed all seams with foil tape. Patched the inspection hole with drywall, primed with KILZ, and painted.
notes: The trilogy is complete. Three years, three ceiling stains, three paint jobs. This time Mike cut an access hole to actually fix it properly instead of just painting over it. The pipe insulation in the ceiling cavity was actually making things worse by holding moisture against the drywall. Installed proper vapor-barrier insulation. If this stain comes back again I am going to lose my mind.

description: Water is leaking under the bathroom sink. Again. Under the sink, the PVC joint is dripping steadily. I put a bucket under it but it fills up in a couple hours. This is the third time this exact thing has happened.
resolution: Bathroom sink PVC joint failure — third time at the same location under the sink. The P-trap compression fitting cracked at the exact same stress point as the previous two failures (January 2022 and January 2024). The drain stack misalignment is putting approximately 15 degrees of lateral force on this joint, which exceeds the fatigue limit of standard PVC compression fittings over time. Replaced the P-trap assembly with a heavier-gauge fitting and added a pipe support bracket to reduce stress. I'm giving you a loyalty discount at this point. Mike recommended full section replacement of the drain line from the sink to the stack to eliminate the misalignment, but owner deferred. Again.
notes: PVC joint under the bathroom sink failed for the THIRD TIME. Same joint, same failure mode, same root cause. Mike recommended full section replacement but owner deferred. Mike's invoice literally says 'I'm giving you a loyalty discount at this point.' The pipe support bracket might buy us more time but until the drain stack misalignment is corrected, this will keep happening. Cost of repeated repairs now exceeds what the proper fix would have cost in 2022.

description: Smoke detector false alarm again. I was searing a steak on high heat and the detector went off. The photoelectric one you installed is better than the old one but my cast iron skillet at 500 degrees apparently produces enough smoke to set off anything.
resolution: Smoke detector is working as designed — tenant's cooking technique produces legitimate smoke that any detector would pick up. Moved the detector an additional 3 feet further from the kitchen to the absolute maximum distance allowed by code. Also installed a small magnetic detector cover plate that the tenant can temporarily deploy while cooking (must be removed immediately after). Instructed tenant on proper use.
notes: Third false alarm. The tenant is a serious home cook and this apartment has no real kitchen ventilation. The smoke detector is now as far from the kitchen as building code will allow. Rosa says the real solution is a proper range hood with exterior vent, which would cost about $600-$800. Owner: 'Can't we just move the detector further?' Rosa: 'It's at the legal limit.' Owner: 'Oh.'

description: The toilet is running again. I can hear it cycling. I know it's the flapper, you know it's the flapper, Mike knows it's the flapper. Can we please just get a new toilet this time?
resolution: Removed the 1998 American Standard toilet that has been the bane of our existence for the past 3.5 years. At this point it's cheaper to start fresh. Installed a new Toto Drake II (model CST454CEFG) — a 1.28 GPF high-efficiency toilet with a CEFIONTECT glaze that resists mineral buildup. New wax ring, new supply line, new shut-off valve. Everything is new. This toilet saga is over.
notes: THE TOILET IS FINALLY REPLACED. After 6 repairs over 3.5 years totaling $720 in maintenance costs (flapper $85, fill valve $120, wax ring $250, handle/chain $95, flapper again $85, flapper again $85), the owner finally approved a replacement. A new toilet costs $450 installed. The math has not been in our favor for a while. Mike's invoice says 'at this point it's cheaper to start fresh.' He's right. The old toilet has been ceremonially discarded. Good riddance.

description: An outlet in the second bedroom fell out of the wall when I unplugged a lamp. Like, the whole outlet and the blue box behind it just came out of the wall. There are wires hanging out. I'm not touching anything.
resolution: Old-work electrical box had lost its grip on the drywall — the tabs that secure it had snapped off from age and repeated plug insertions/removals. The outlet was hanging by its wires. Installed a new old-work box with Madison straps for a secure mount, re-terminated the wiring (aluminum with copper pigtails, properly Noaloxed), and installed a new tamper-resistant receptacle. Outlet is now secure and properly grounded.
notes: An outlet literally fell out of the wall. Rosa was less surprised than she should have been. The aluminum-to-copper pigtails were intact and properly done, which is one of the few electrical bright spots in this unit. She says the boxes throughout the unit are the original flimsy kind that don't hold up to modern use.

description: The bedroom window draft is back and worse than before. The weatherstripping we installed in 2022 has come loose and there's a cold breeze coming through. The condensation between the panes is worse too — I can't even see through the window clearly on cold mornings.
resolution: Previous weatherstrip had degraded and partially detached after 2.5 years. Removed old weatherstrip, cleaned the channel, and installed new V-strip weatherstripping along both side jambs and the meeting rail. Applied fresh exterior caulk along the frame perimeter. The IGU condensation is worsening and the window really does need replacement, but the draft should be manageable for another winter.
notes: Second window seal repair. The weatherstripping is a band-aid — the window needs to be replaced entirely. IGU condensation means the seal is completely failed. Window replacement quoted at $650 for a vinyl double-hung. Owner continues to defer. The tenant is spending more on heating due to the draft than the window would cost to replace.

description: Heater won't ignite. Same as November 2021. Apartment is 51 degrees. It's been a particularly cold week for San Francisco and I can see my breath indoors. The unit clicks like it's trying to start but nothing lights.
resolution: Wall heater gas valve solenoid stuck closed — the unit was sparking but no gas was flowing. Disassembled the gas valve, cleaned the solenoid plunger of corrosion and debris, and reinstalled. Gas flow restored and burner ignited on first attempt. Tested through three complete heating cycles. Supply air temperature reaching 125F. However, the gas valve is showing its age and may fail fully in the near future.
notes: James cleaned the gas valve and got it working but warned that the solenoid is corroded and may fail completely next winter. A new gas valve for this model (if it's even still available) would be $400-600. A new wall heater would be $1,500-2,000 installed. James said 'this heater is older than most of my employees.'

description: The bathroom sink is draining slowly now — takes about 3 minutes for a full basin to empty. After all the pipe work under there I'm paranoid about anything water-related in this bathroom.
resolution: Bathroom sink drain line had buildup of hair, soap, and toothpaste residue approximately 2 feet past the P-trap. Snaked with a 1/4-inch drum auger and cleared the blockage. Flushed with hot water and enzyme cleaner. Drain now empties a full basin in approximately 15 seconds. Inspected the P-trap connection (the infamous one) — holding fine with the support bracket installed in October.
notes: Standard drain clog, unrelated to the ongoing PVC saga. Mike checked the P-trap joint while he was there — still holding. He seems genuinely relieved. The support bracket appears to be doing its job. Small victories.

description: The garbage disposal is making that grinding noise AGAIN and now there's a burning electrical smell coming from under the sink. I turned it off immediately. This is the fourth time this disposal has had problems. I'm convinced it's cursed.
resolution: Garbage disposal motor is burned out — the unit was drawing excessive amperage and the thermal overload tripped. After resetting, the motor ran briefly then produced the burning smell and tripped again. The intermittent grinding noise that has plagued this unit for years was likely caused by a worn impeller plate that finally seized. Replaced the entire disposal with a new InSinkErator Evolution Compact 3/4 HP unit. Connected to dishwasher drain, tested with water flow and food waste. Running smooth and quiet.
notes: Fourth disposal incident. The haunted disposal has finally died for real. The burning smell was the motor windings giving up. Mike installed a nicer 3/4 HP model this time — 'if we're going to do this, let's do it right.' The ghost in the disposal has been exorcised. For now.

description: Kitchen GFCI tripping again when I run the new garbage disposal. It worked fine for a few weeks after installation but now every time I turn on the disposal the outlet trips.
resolution: New garbage disposal has a higher starting current than the old unit (3/4 HP vs 1/2 HP) and the momentary inrush is tripping the GFCI. The disposal is on the same GFCI-protected circuit as the kitchen counter outlets. Installed a new 20A GFCI outlet with a higher trip threshold that can handle motor inrush current. Also tightened the neutral connections which were contributing to nuisance tripping. Disposal now runs without tripping the GFCI.
notes: Consequence of upgrading the disposal without upgrading the electrical. The bigger motor draws more starting current. Rosa says the kitchen electrical was barely adequate for the old appliances and any upgrades are going to push it over the edge. This is what happens when you improve one thing in a 60-year-old building.

description: The shower is dripping constantly from the showerhead even when turned off. It's not a lot but it drips about once every 2 seconds. The handle is also getting harder to turn.
resolution: Shower mixing valve cartridge was worn — the internal seals were no longer holding against water pressure in the off position. Shut off water supply, removed the handle and trim, and extracted the old Moen 1222 cartridge (which required a cartridge puller due to mineral buildup in the valve body). Installed a new Moen 1222 replacement cartridge and new handle trim. Shower turns off completely — no drip. Handle action is smooth.
notes: Standard shower valve cartridge replacement. The Moen 1222 is a common cartridge and relatively easy to replace — when it's not cemented in by mineral deposits. Mike had to use the puller tool for 10 minutes to extract the old one. SF water hardness strikes again.

description: The bathroom door won't close properly. It swings open on its own and doesn't latch. The top corner of the door scrapes against the frame when you try to push it closed. I have to lean against it to get it to stay shut.
resolution: Bathroom door hinges have loosened over time — the top hinge screw holes in the door frame are stripped, causing the door to sag and bind against the frame at the top corner. Filled the stripped screw holes with wooden dowels and wood glue, let cure overnight, then re-drilled pilot holes and re-mounted the hinge with longer 3-inch screws that bite into the wall stud. Planed 1/8 inch from the top edge of the door to provide clearance. Door now swings freely and latches properly.
notes: Door sagging due to stripped hinge screws. Very common in older buildings with softwood frames. The dowel-and-reglue method is the standard fix. Mike is basically the general handyman for this unit at this point — he does plumbing, doors, windows, drywall, and probably therapy.

description: You have got to be kidding me. The AC is dead. Again. Third summer, third capacitor. The hum-but-no-start thing. James predicted this. I need him here before I melt.
resolution: Start/run capacitor failed — third year in a row. Same failure, same cause. I'm going to start stockpiling these. The west-facing condenser location bakes the capacitor in afternoon sun, and the standard-rated capacitor can't handle the thermal cycling. This time I installed a higher-rated 40/5 MFD capacitor (up from 35/5) with a 440V continuous duty rating, which should handle the extra thermal stress better. Also installed a UV-resistant shade panel over the condenser unit to reduce direct solar heating. This one should last longer.
notes: Third summer, third capacitor failure. James is now on first-name basis with this AC unit. His invoice says 'I'm going to start stockpiling these.' He took it upon himself to install a shade panel over the condenser — didn't even charge for it. He says if the uprated capacitor and shade panel don't break the cycle, the condenser unit needs to be relocated to the north side of the building, which would cost significantly more.

description: There's a steady drip behind the kitchen wall — I can hear it when it's quiet. No visible water yet but there's a damp spot forming on the wall behind the stove, about 2 feet up from the floor.
resolution: Hot water supply line behind the kitchen wall had a pinhole leak at a galvanized-to-copper transition fitting. The dissimilar metals caused galvanic corrosion over time, eventually creating a leak. Cut out the corroded section and installed a dielectric union to properly join the copper and galvanized sections. Patched the drywall access hole. Tested at full pressure — no leak.
notes: Galvanic corrosion between dissimilar metals — a classic problem in older buildings where copper piping was added to galvanized systems without proper dielectric unions. Mike says there are probably other galvanized-to-copper transitions throughout the unit that will eventually develop the same problem. Full repipe would be $4,000-$6,000. Added to the growing list of 'things the owner is thinking about.'

description: The washing machine shakes violently during the spin cycle. Like, it walks across the floor. Last night it banged against the wall so hard the picture in the living room fell off. It also smells mildewy inside the drum.
resolution: Washing machine leveling feet were unevenly adjusted, and one of the four shock absorbers (suspension rods) was broken, causing the drum to oscillate wildly during high-speed spin. Replaced the broken shock absorber, re-leveled all four feet, and confirmed the machine is sitting flat. Also cleaned the drum gasket of mildew buildup and ran a hot sanitize cycle with affresh tablets. Machine now runs through spin cycle without excessive vibration.
notes: The washing machine literally shook a picture off the wall in the next room. Broken shock absorber was the culprit. Mike also addressed the mildew issue — front-load washers are notorious for gasket mildew if the door is kept closed between uses. Advised tenant to leave the door ajar.

description: The kitchen ceiling light started flickering again — same kind of flicker as when the fridge compressor kicks on, but now it does it randomly even when the fridge isn't running. Sometimes the light just dims for a second then comes back.
resolution: Traced the intermittent flickering to a loose neutral wire at the panel bus bar — same issue identified in February 2023 but worsened over time. Re-torqued the neutral bus bar connection to spec. Also found oxidation on the main neutral lug — cleaned with Noalox and re-torqued. Flickering resolved. This panel continues to be a concern.
notes: Rosa re-torqued the neutral lug she tightened in 2023. It loosened again because the Federal Pacific bus bar is known for poor terminal grip. She says this is her last warning about this panel — if it's not replaced within 6 months, she's going to recommend the tenant contact the building inspector. She's not wrong.

description: I found mouse droppings under the kitchen sink and in the cabinet next to the stove. Also found a small gap where the gas line enters the wall behind the stove. I'm pretty sure that's where they're getting in.
resolution: Sealed three penetration gaps around the kitchen: gas line wall entry (1/2 inch gap), cold water supply line entry (3/8 inch gap), and a crack at the baseboard where it meets the floor (1/4 inch gap). Used steel wool packed into the gaps and sealed with expanding foam for the pipe penetrations, and caulked the baseboard gap. Cleaned and sanitized the affected cabinet areas. Set two snap traps behind the stove as a precaution.
notes: Classic SF rodent entry — gaps around pipe penetrations. The 1960s construction has settled enough to create gaps that weren't there originally. Steel wool and foam is the standard fix. Set traps as well. Welcome to city living.

description: The new toilet (the Toto) is fine, but the shut-off valve under it is dripping slightly. Just a tiny bead of water forming at the valve handle.
resolution: Supply shut-off valve packing nut was slightly loose — tightened 1/4 turn with an adjustable wrench and the drip stopped immediately. This is a common issue with new shut-off valves after initial installation — the packing can settle slightly. No parts needed, no charge for the return visit.
notes: No charge — Mike said it's a warranty callback on his November 2024 toilet install. Just needed a quick tightening. The new Toto is performing beautifully. Not a single toilet issue since the replacement. Should have done this years ago.

description: The oven isn't heating to the right temperature. I set it to 400 degrees but my oven thermometer says it only reaches about 325. My cookies came out doughy and my pizza was sad. Something is definitely off.
resolution: Oven temperature sensor (thermistor) was reading high resistance, causing the control board to think the oven was hotter than it actually was and shutting off the heating element prematurely. Replaced the oven temperature sensor and recalibrated the thermostat. Oven now reaches and holds 400F within 3 degrees accuracy. Ran a 30-minute burn test to confirm consistent temperature.
notes: Oven temperature sensor failure. Simple fix but it explains why the tenant's baking has been 'off' lately. The range is about 12 years old — sensors drift over time. Should be accurate now.

description: Heater is making a strange woofing sound when it ignites — like a mini explosion — then runs normally. It scares me every time it kicks on. There's also a faint gas smell near the unit before it ignites.
resolution: Delayed ignition caused by partially clogged burner ports — gas was accumulating before the igniter could light it, causing the 'woofing' sound. This is a safety hazard as it can damage the heat exchanger. Removed the burner assembly, cleaned all burner ports with a wire brush and compressed air, and adjusted the gas-air mixture shutter. Tested through five ignition cycles — immediate, clean ignition with no gas accumulation. Also checked for heat exchanger cracks with a visual inspection and combustion analyzer — CO readings are within normal limits.
notes: The delayed ignition 'woof' is a sign the burner isn't getting enough air or the ports are clogged. James cleaned it up and it's working properly now. He checked for heat exchanger cracks while he was there — clear for now, but he says the unit's days are numbered. This heater is approaching antique status.

description: The water heater is leaking from the bottom of the tank. There's a steady stream of rusty water coming from under the unit and the closet is flooding. The tank is making popping and crackling sounds. It woke me up at 4:30am.
resolution: Water heater tank has failed — the bottom of the tank has rusted through from the inside due to years of sediment accumulation and a depleted anode rod. Tank is leaking rusty water at approximately 1 gallon per hour. This is not repairable — the tank must be replaced. Shut off the water supply and gas to the unit, drained the remaining tank, and cleaned up the water. Installed a new 40-gallon Rheem Performance Plus gas water heater with a 12-year warranty. Includes new flexible gas connector, water supply lines, expansion tank (transferring the one we installed in 2022), and updated T&P relief valve discharge line to code.
notes: The water heater Mike has been warning about since December 2022 has finally died. It lasted 18 years — 6 years past its expected lifespan. The owner paid $1,450 for the emergency replacement when a planned replacement last year would have been $1,200 and wouldn't have involved a 4:30am emergency call. Mike's exact words: 'I told you in 2022 this needed replacing.' He did. We have it in writing.

description: The hallway floor creaks terribly — like haunted house level creaking. Every step makes a loud squeak. It's worst near the bathroom door. The floor also feels slightly bouncy in that area, like it gives a little when you walk on it.
resolution: Hallway subfloor has developed squeaks due to nail pops and minor subfloor deflection between joists. The bounciness near the bathroom door is related to minor moisture damage to the subfloor from years of bathroom plumbing issues. Secured the subfloor to the joists with 2.5-inch deck screws driven through the existing flooring and subfloor into the joists (located with a stud finder and confirmed with test holes). Installed 10 screws to eliminate the worst squeaks. The bathroom-area softness is not a structural concern yet but should be monitored.
notes: The cumulative effect of 5 years of plumbing leaks in the bathroom is starting to show in the hallway subfloor. Not critical yet but the bouncy spot near the bathroom is a warning sign. If the PVC drain fails again (or anything else leaks in that bathroom), the subfloor will need to be torn out and replaced — a $2,000-$3,000 project. Every deferred repair in this unit is connected to every other deferred repair. It's the circle of neglect.

description: The whole apartment lost power during a storm. I went to the breaker panel and three breakers had tripped. When I tried to reset them, one of them (the one for the kitchen) made a loud pop and wouldn't stay on. There was a brief spark visible inside the panel.
resolution: Three breakers tripped during a power surge from the storm. Breaker #3 (kitchen circuit) arced internally when the tenant attempted to reset it — this is the third Federal Pacific Stab-Lok breaker failure in this panel. The breaker is stuck in a partially-on state and cannot be safely reset. Replaced the failed breaker with an aftermarket replacement. All other breakers tested and reset successfully. However, this panel is now OFFICIALLY a safety hazard. I am documenting this as a formal safety recommendation: this Federal Pacific Stab-Lok panel must be replaced. I will not be comfortable servicing individual breakers in this panel going forward.
notes: Rosa is done being diplomatic. Her report for this visit is essentially an ultimatum — replace the panel or she will advise the tenant to contact the SF Department of Building Inspection. She has documented the Federal Pacific panel's failure history across three years and multiple breaker failures. The panel replacement is quoted at $3,500 and the owner has finally scheduled it for next month. Only took a visible arc and a spark to get it approved. Better late than never. Total maintenance cost for this unit since 2021: approximately $12,500. About 40% of that could have been avoided with proactive repairs. This property is a comedy of deferred maintenance.
